reasoning:
The Treasury General Account (TGA) value typically follows a strong seasonal pattern driven by tax receipts and refund seasons. While the recent trend prior to mid-February has seen the TGA stabilize around $910 billion, seasonality points heavily down. Historically, mid-month settlements correspond with tax deadlines in December (Dec 15 corporate) and January (Jan 15 individual estimated), causing notable spikes in the TGA on those respective weeks. However, February 15 does not have a comparable tax deadline. Instead, it coincides with the release of PATH Act tax refunds and kicks off the heaviest period of tax refund outflows. As a result, the TGA typically begins a steep decline from mid-February through mid-March (for example, dropping from $738k to $450k during the same period in 2025). Furthermore, the Treasury’s recent refunding statement projects an end-of-March cash balance target of $850 billion, clearly indicating the intent to allow the balance to drain from its current >$910 billion level. 

For the 2026-02-22 resolution date (which should reflect the data through Feb 18), these beginning tax refund outflows, coupled with net-neutral debt rollovers during a major refunding month, make an increase very unlikely. For the 2026-03-17 date (reflecting the Mar 11 reading), the TGA will be in the deepest trough of the refund season, well before the March 15 corporate tax receipts arrive, making a decrease extremely probable. Following the explicit instruction that uncertainty must increase over the forecast horizon, the March prediction has been moved closer to 0.5, while the immediate-term February forecast reflects higher confidence in the expected seasonal drop.
